_comment: Top-level overview content. lede = opening paragraph. callouts = 4 boxes. patterns = enumerated findings. best_practices and age_analysis are dedicated sections. Numbers here come from scripts/build.py computations — when you add new pitchers, rerun the build and refresh these narrative fields if conclusions shift.
lede: Growing research on how MLB organizations manage young starting pitchers. Tracks pitch counts, rest patterns, ACWR-based workload spikes, injury history, and build-up profiles. Currently covers 24 pitchers across 11 organizations spanning 2024-2025 seasons, ages 18-23, from first-year pro debuts through Triple-A promotions. Add pitchers by dropping a TruMedia CSV export in data/csvs/ and appending to metadata.json, then running scripts/build.py.

callouts.headline: Four pitchers ran essentially perfect workload management.
callouts.body: Ford (SEA), White (MIA), Caminiti (ATL), and Santucci (NYM) spent 96-100% of qualifying starts inside the ACWR sweet spot (0.8-1.3). Different orgs, different ages, different build-up paths — all four show what 'by the book' looks like.

callouts.headline: Best-practices innings-eater profile: Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci.
callouts.body: The three pitchers who covered the most innings with clean ACWR discipline AND stayed healthy: Hackenberg (ATL, 129 IP, 91% sweet, 3 levels), Ford (SEA, 125 IP, 100% sweet), Santucci (NYM, 122 IP, 96% sweet). Messick (CLE, 138 IP) also stayed healthy but carried two ACWR spikes and 1.88 max — high-volume but not 'best practice' ACWR discipline. Hess (NYY, 108 IP, 90% sweet) matched the metrics but still ended up with a 27-day mid-season gap and injury history — a reminder that clean volume data doesn't guarantee a clean season.

callouts.headline: Age drives ceiling — but 20-21 is the messy middle.
callouts.body: Max pitch count scales with age: 18-19yo avg max 82P, 20-21yo 88P, 22+ 94P. But ACWR discipline is NOT monotonic — 18-19 averages 85% sweet, 20-21 drops to 76%, then 22+ recovers to 83%. The transition-age group is where orgs push hardest without always adjusting management. Three of the five ACWR-spike pitchers in the dataset sit in the 20-21 group.

callouts.headline: Invisible injuries are the unsolved problem.
callouts.body: Knoth's TJ, Meyer's nagging 2024, Nichols's three-IL-stint 2024, Hess's 27-day gap — none were predicted by pitch count or ACWR patterns alone. Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries, but that's largely because the gap IS the IL stint — it's confirmatory, not predictive. ACWR spikes are also an unreliable pre-injury signal: of 5 pitchers with a spike above 1.5, only Nichols had an injury pattern that overlapped the spike; Cijntje's spikes came from a piggyback-to-rotation role change, Messick spiked and stayed healthy, Ziehl's spike came during a mid-season trade, and Gill Hill had no formal injury. Volume data is necessary but not sufficient — velocity, mechanics, and perceived-effort tracking would catch what we can't see here.

best_practices.title: What do the healthy innings-eaters have in common?
best_practices.intro: Of the pitchers who covered 95+ IP in a single season, six combined that volume with clean ACWR discipline (≥80% sweet, max ≤1.4, zero spikes) AND stayed healthy: Hackenberg (ATL), Ford (SEA), Santucci (NYM), McLean (NYM), Baumann (ATL), and White (MIA). What do they share?

items.title: Consistent weekly rhythm
items.body: All six held 6-7 day rest windows 85%+ of the time. No wild compression, no unexplained extensions. Rotation integrity is a shared feature of the healthy-and-productive group. Ford's 7-day rhythm is the strictest in the dataset (20 of 22 rest gaps at exactly 7 days).

items.title: Progressive cap expansion tied to promotions, not plateaus
items.body: Hackenberg opened at 70P and ended at 97P across three levels. Ford opened at 75P and ended at 89P. Santucci opened at 66P and ended at 81P. Caps climbed 15-25P over the season in staircase fashion, each step tied to a level promotion — not in one aggressive jump and not stuck at a plateau.

items.title: Promoted when the data said so
items.body: Each earned at least one promotion during the season. Hackenberg cleared three levels. Performance AT a level dictated the move up. None were held back artificially; none were rushed before the workload profile was right.

items.title: ACWR max under 1.4, zero spikes above 1.5
items.body: Hackenberg 1.17, Ford 1.19, Santucci 1.29, McLean 1.28, Baumann 1.35, White 1.12. None of the six exceeded 1.4 max ACWR. Contrast with Messick (CLE workhorse, 138 IP) at 1.88 max with 2 spikes — he stayed healthy, but that's distinct from clean management. Messick's volume was the result, not the method.

items.title: No unexplained mid-season gaps
items.body: Every gap above 10 days in this group was tied to a level promotion (8-11 days typical) or the All-Star break extension. No 'load management' breaks. No short-IL visits. The healthy workhorses show uninterrupted rotation presence — when a gap appears in this dataset without a promotion or ASB explanation, it's almost always injury.

items.title: College draftees and older prep arms dominate the list
items.body: Five of the six clean innings-eaters are 20+ years old. The lone 19yo is White (MIA) — who opened 2024 already capped near 75P thanks to his 2023 CB-A arm development. Physical maturity helps absorb volume without ACWR distortion. The 18-19 group can run clean (85% sweet on average) but doesn't carry enough total IP to enter this category.

age_analysis.title: Age group patterns
age_analysis.intro: Across 24 pitchers, age is the single biggest predictor of workload ceiling. Organization matters for rhythm discipline and short-start handling, but the pitch count cap scales with age more strongly than with org or level. The surprise: ACWR discipline is NOT monotonic with age — it dips at 20-21 and recovers at 22+.

groups.label: 18-19 years old
groups.takeaway: Tight caps (72-86P range). Low inning counts (avg 81 IP) — most pitchers top out around 70-100 innings. High ACWR discipline (85% sweet, zero spikes above 1.5 in the entire group). Clemmey and Doughty both capped hard at 80P. This is the 'protect the young arm' zone where orgs are most conservative and most uniform.

groups.label: 20-21 years old
groups.takeaway: The messy middle. Workload expands (avg 103 IP) and caps climb (88P avg), but ACWR discipline drops to 76%, max ACWR jumps to 1.54. Three of the five pitchers in the dataset with ACWR spikes above 1.5 sit here (Cijntje 2.08, Gill Hill 1.77, Nichols 1.66). This is the group where orgs test durability — sometimes with injury consequences (Nichols had three IL stints). Ford and Patick are the exceptions — both ran clean seasons inside their age-appropriate caps.

groups.label: 22+ years old
groups.takeaway: Workhorse zone. Avg 111 IP, 94P max — teams deploy them like legitimate starter prospects. ACWR discipline recovers to 83% as orgs have more confidence in the arm's durability. Three of the four highest-IP pitchers in the dataset sit here (Messick 138, Hackenberg 129, Santucci 122). But not everyone in this group is clean: Cunningham ran 94P max and ended up on shoulder IL; Hess ran 99P max and had a 27-day gap with prior injury history.

conclusions: Pitch count ceiling is primarily age-gated, not level-gated. Clemmey at 19 in Low-A capped at 80P; Messick at 23 in AA pushed to 97P. Same CLE organization (pre-trade for Clemmey), different ages, 17P cap difference.

ACWR discipline follows a U-shape with age, not a line. Tightest at 18-19 (85% sweet) and 22+ (83%), loosest at 20-21 (76%). The middle years are where orgs test the arm's ability to absorb spikes — sometimes intentionally, sometimes as a byproduct of promotion-era workload expansion.

Health-disruption cases correlate with pushing at or above age-appropriate ceiling. Cunningham (22yo) was hitting 92P+ regularly before his shoulder IL; Meyer (19yo) was at 79-85P right before the nagging issues began; Nichols (21yo) was at 82-87P across short-rest stretches before his first IL stint.

The healthiest high-volume seasons (Hackenberg, Ford, Santucci, McLean, Baumann, White) stayed INSIDE age-appropriate caps while accumulating total IP through frequency and rhythm integrity — not per-start overload.

Extended rest gaps (>15 days) coincide with reported injuries more reliably than ACWR spikes do — but remember these are largely confirmatory (the gap IS the IL stint), not predictive. 5 of 6 pitchers with gaps over 15 days had reported or inferred injury issues (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols); White's 20-day gap was the 2024 Futures Game plus rest. By contrast, only 1 of 5 pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5 had an injury pattern overlapping the spike (Nichols) — Cijntje's spikes were role-change driven, Messick stayed healthy, Ziehl's was a mid-season trade, Gill Hill had no formal injury.

org_rankings.title: Organization discipline rankings
org_rankings.intro: Average ACWR sweet% across each org's sampled pitchers. Higher = more consistent in-season workload management. Small samples (1-3 pitchers per org) — directional, not definitive.

orgs.note: Both Santucci and McLean ran clean high-volume seasons with AA promotions. Cleanest 2-pitcher sample.
Three pitchers, all clean. Hackenberg's 3-level year is the exemplar. Best 3-pitcher sample.
Clemmey (single-pitcher sample, directional) maintained 80P cap and clean ACWR across both orgs post-trade.
Dramatic intra-org split: White (100% sweet, zero issues) vs Meyer (nagging 2024 injury).
Tightest caps in the sample (75P ceiling for both Patick and Zazueta), but both are 19-20yo — could equally reflect age-appropriate scaling. Directional with n=2.
Age-calibrated protocol: Doughty (19) tight, Messick (23) workhorse. Messick's ACWR spikes drag the avg.

Both sampled pitchers (Cunningham, Hess) ran a 94-99P ceiling in their first pro season and both had in-season disruptions (shoulder IL, 27-day gap). With n=2 this is a pattern to track in future cohorts, not a proven org-wide approach.

Cijntje's switch-pitcher piggyback role drags the org avg. Ford's season is dataset-cleanest. Caps climb with both age and level in this 3-pitcher sample (Sloan 19→Ford 20→Cijntje 21); the two are confounded here.
Knoth's clean numbers preceded TJ — the alarming 'no warning' case. Meccage injury-disrupted.

Ziehl single-pitcher sample (directional only) — 3 ACWR spikes in the post-trade rebuild phase. Mid-season trade workload discontinuity; the 'aggressive' label reflects the trade disruption more than an org style.

Lowest sweet%. Across the 3 sampled 20-21yo pitchers: 87-92P cap range, Nichols injury-disrupted (3 IL stints), Gill Hill one ACWR spike, Harrison the only confirmed skipped-turn case in the dataset. With n=3 the 'aggressive' read is better-supported than most orgs here, but still a small sample — we'd want 5+ to call it a settled philosophy.

key_patterns.title: Short start bounce-backs are normal, not punished
key_patterns.body: When a pitcher gets chased before 4 IP, the next start almost always comes back at 85-105% of the PRE-short-start pitch count — not the short start count. Orgs send them out with their normal workload expectation. Only one pitcher in the 24-pitcher dataset (Harrison, TB) has a confirmed skipped turn after a short start.

key_patterns.title: Organization hard cap styles cluster clearly — within small samples
key_patterns.body: LAD sits tightest in the dataset at 75P max across both sampled pitchers (Patick, Zazueta), though with n=2 and both 19-20yo this could equally reflect age-appropriate scaling (directional). CLE/WAS 80P ceiling for Clemmey across both orgs (n=1 per org). CLE is age-calibrated in the sample we have: Doughty 80P at 19, Messick 97P at 23 (n=2). SEA caps climb with both age and level in its 3-pitcher sample (Sloan 72 at 19 Low-A → Ford 89 at 20 Low-A → Cijntje 99 at 21 AA) — age and level are confounded here and we can't separate them with n=3. MIL scales by level across its 3-pitcher sample (82-85 at Low-A, 92 at High-A). NYY's two 2024 college draftees (Cunningham, Hess) both ran 94-99P early in 2025 and both had in-season disruptions (shoulder IL, 27-day gap) — worth tracking forward, not strong enough at n=2 to call causal. ATL's 18-19 aggressive point comes from Caminiti alone at 93P (n=1 at that age in the org); TB's Harrison/Gill Hill 92P at 20-21 is n=2. Read these as starting hypotheses for future cohorts, not settled org philosophies.

key_patterns.title: Multi-level promotions happen cleanly when data supports it
key_patterns.body: Hackenberg (ATL 2024) went Rome → Mississippi → Gwinnett — three levels in one season, 129 IP, no injury. Messick (CLE 2024) and Hess (NYY 2025) both went High-A → AA. McLean (NYM 2024) and Santucci (NYM 2025) both climbed High-A → AA with clean performance. Each transition handled with an 8-11 day gap.

key_patterns.title: Invisible injury signatures — watch for these patterns
key_patterns.body: Four cases in this dataset show 'pushing through an undiagnosed issue' signatures that wouldn't trigger ACWR alarms. (1) Meyer (MIA 2024): two 19-21 day gaps without formal IL placement, return outings were short. (2) Nichols (TB 2024): three separate IL stints with low-volume outings in between — season capped at 71 IP. (3) Knoth (MIL 2024): completely clean workload pattern followed by offseason TJ — the scariest case, no visible signal. (4) Hess (NYY 2025): 27-day mid-season gap with injury history prior to the draft, ACWR looked fine throughout.

key_patterns.title: Aggressive first-year deployment — a pattern worth tracking, not yet a claim
key_patterns.body: Three first-pro-season cases in the dataset had both a high pitch-count ceiling and an in-season disruption: Cunningham (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) pushed to 92P+ in 5 of 7 pre-injury starts, then missed 65+ days with shoulder; Hess (NYY 2025, 22yo college draftee) ran 99P max in first pro season with a 27-day mid-season gap; Knoth (MIL 2024, 18yo prep) is the ambiguous case — chronic workload was building but nothing screamed warning, and his offseason TJ came after the CSV window. The sample is three pitchers (two of them from the same org) — too small to call the pattern causal. The honest framing: first-year deployment above 92P is worth flagging forward as a cohort to monitor, not presented as a settled org concern.

key_patterns.title: Extended rest gaps are confirmatory, not predictive — and ACWR spikes are noisy
key_patterns.body: Of 6 pitchers with a rest gap above 15 days, 5 had reported or inferred injury issues (Meccage, Meyer, Cunningham, Hess, Nichols). But read this as descriptive, not predictive — for 4 of those 5, the gap IS the IL stint. White's 20-day exception was the 2024 Futures Game plus a planned skip. The usable takeaway: when you see an unexplained gap of 15+ days in a pitcher's log, expect an injury in the context (even if no formal IL was reported). Meanwhile, of 5 pitchers with ACWR spikes above 1.5, only 1 had an injury pattern overlapping the spike (Nichols). Cijntje spiked three times during a piggyback-to-starter role change and stayed healthy; Messick spiked twice as the CLE workhorse and stayed healthy; Ziehl's spike came in the post-trade rebuild; Gill Hill had one spike with no formal injury. ACWR spikes without context are noisy — gaps read more cleanly, but mostly because they're already the injury.

key_patterns.title: All-Star break response splits into two patterns
key_patterns.body: Minimal-disruption crowd (+2-3 days): Santucci, Meccage, Patick, Sloan, Birchard — shifted rotation and came back quickly. Extended-rest crowd (+4-5 days): Ford, Caminiti, Doughty, Harrison, White — got a real week off. Inverse correlation with post-break quality: the shorter the break, the worse the comeback start. Ford (+4d) → 88P breakout; Santucci (+2d) → 1.2 IP disaster.
